coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-functionalized polyaniline incorporated into sodium alginate/cellulose nanofibrils film for sustainable intelligent and active packaging of shrimp

coredata.dc:description: A novel active colorimetric indicator was designed as a smart food packaging material by incorporating eco-friendly carbon dot functionalized polyaniline complex (CD@PANI) into sodium alginate/cellulose nanofibrils (SA/CNF) polymer matrix. The addition of CD@PANI to SA/CNF polymer enhanced the network structure and tensile strength (20 %) through non-covalent binding with the film-forming matrix, decreased UV light transmittance (88.0 % for UV-A and 91.5 % for UV-B), and exhibited a visually recognizable colorimetric response from green to blue depending on pH change and ammonia vapors. SA/CNF/CD@PANI film showed the highest antioxidant power of 100 % for ABTS and 69.2 % for DPPH radical scavenging assays, alongside robust antimicrobial efficacy against common foodborne pathogens. In addition, the films were applied to shrimp packaging and demonstrated a pronounced ability to indicate product freshness through visual color change, and effectively prolonged the freshness of shrimp under storage conditions. The proposed multifunctional packaging film provides a novel approach to enhance food safety and track the freshness of perishable products.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-functionalized polyaniline incorporated into sodium alginate/cellulose nanofibrils film for sustainable intelligent and active packaging of shrimp

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel active colorimetric indicator was designed as a smart food packaging material by incorporating eco-friendly carbon dot functionalized polyaniline complex (CD@PANI) into sodium alginate/cellulose nanofibrils (SA/CNF) polymer matrix. The addition of CD@PANI to SA/CNF polymer enhanced the network structure and tensile strength (20 %) through non-covalent binding with the film-forming matrix, decreased UV light transmittance (88.0 % for UV-A and 91.5 % for UV-B), and exhibited a visually recognizable colorimetric response from green to blue depending on pH change and ammonia vapors. SA/CNF/CD@PANI film showed the highest antioxidant power of 100 % for ABTS and 69.2 % for DPPH radical scavenging assays, alongside robust antimicrobial efficacy against common foodborne pathogens. In addition, the films were applied to shrimp packaging and demonstrated a pronounced ability to indicate product freshness through visual color change, and effectively prolonged the freshness of shrimp under storage conditions. The proposed multifunctional packaging film provides a novel approach to enhance food safety and track the freshness of perishable products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety is a critical global issue exacerbated by the substantial food waste that contributes to safety and environmental problems. To meet the demand for rapid and accurate detection of food spoilage and mitigation of food waste, recent studies have concentrated on the development of active intelligent packaging films [1]. Active packaging materials improve quality and safety by preventing food deterioration during storage. On the other hand, intelligent packaging materials are capable of detecting and reporting changes in food quality, freshness, or safety throughout the storage period [2]. Packaging materials that integrate both active and intelligent components offer an innovative dual-function approach that is imperative to strengthen safety during transportation, preservation and usage of food products [3].

Spoiled foods such as shrimp, meat, fish, and crab can release organic amines, which can cause pH changes in the surrounding environment. Therefore, monitoring pH variations is a practical and straightforward method for studying food spoilage [4]. Currently, various natural pigments, such as curcumin, betaine, and anthocyanins, have been utilized in the development of pH-responsive intelligent packaging systems [5]. However, natural dyes are susceptible to degradation and color change due to factors such as light exposure, temperature variations, metal ions, and other environmental conditions, which may affect the dye's ability. Additionally, the instability of these dyes increases the potential of dye migration into food products, which may pose a risk to food safety [6]. Hence, there is a need to develop a sensor that offers improved stability, safety, and sensitivity for applications in intelligent food packaging. Polyaniline (PANI) stands out as a promising candidate due to its reliability, rapidity, simplicity, environmental friendliness, and non-leaching nature [7]. PANI is a stable conducting polymer featuring a ring structure with nitrogen-substituted derivatives and is synthesized via oxidative polymerization of aniline monomers [8]. It exists in one of three stable oxidation states, namely pernigraniline, emeraldine, and leucoemeraldine.

Emeraldine salt is green in color, whereas the base form has a blue hue [9]. The transition between emeraldine salt and the base oxidation state of PANI occurs via protonation and deprotonation processes. Therefore, variations in pH (acidic or basic conditions) affect the oxidation state of PANI, leading to observable color changes, making PANI suitable as a colorimetric sensor [10]. Hence, PANI changes color from green to blue in response to meat spoilage-induced metabolites such as total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) in the headspace of the package and pH changes [11]. In this regard, Kuswandi et al. employed PANI film as a colorimetric food packaging label to monitor the freshness of fish products [12]. Wang et al. also reported a PANI-based intelligent colorimetric indicator for detecting the freshness of Tilapia [13]. However, there is currently no report on the packaging application of PANI to meet the emerging demand for simultaneous freshness monitoring and shelf life extension of products. Herein, this study aimed to make a hybrid composite of PANI with carbon dots (CDs) as an active intelligent nanofiller.

CDs are a class of nanomaterials with good aqueous solubility, low toxicity, easy synthesis, antioxidant, antibacterial, and anti-UV characteristics, and have the potential to be used as active fillers [14]. Nowadays, the synthesis of CDs using a green approach, including the use of plant extracts, microorganisms and other waste materials, has attracted attention [15,16]. Cinnamon, an eternal tree in tropical medicine, belongs to the Lauraceae family and is one of the most important spices used daily by people all over the world [17]. Cinnamon primarily contains vital essential oils and other derivatives such as cinnamaldehyde, cinnamic acid, and cinnamate, which can be used as medicinal plant-based CDs. However, there is no study on the use of cinnamon-derived carbon dots for food packaging purposes. Therefore, we first synthesized cinnamon-based carbon dots using a simple hydrothermal method, and then an in situ polymerization of PANI in the presence of CDs solution was performed to prepare a dual active intelligent composite filler (CD@PANI). The formation of CD@PANI composites was investigated by UV–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR)spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and elemental-mapping using EDX-spectrum. Then, the prepared particles (CDs, PANI, and CD@PANI) were incorporated into the sodium alginate/cellulose nanofibers (SA/CNF) blend polymer as a packaging film matrix. Among natural biodegradable polymers such as carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids etc., alginate is an anionic polysaccharide derived from brown algae, which has attracted much attention in the field of food packaging due to its good film-forming ability [18]. Unfortunately, packaging materials made of pure alginate have performance defects such as strong hydrophilicity, weak mechanical properties, and limited packaging functions [19]. Combining high-strength CNF with SA can improve the drawbacks of pure SA. The high specific surface area and nanoscale effect of CNF lead to excellent barrier and mechanical properties, making it a good choice as a reinforcing polymer for SA.

Subsequently, characteristics, physicochemical properties, colorimetric reactivity, and ammonia gas detection of the composite films were comprehensively evaluated. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed packaging system was verified by applying the composite packaging films to preserve and track the freshness of the shrimp. The strategy proposed in this paper aims to provide a novel idea for developing effective and stable dual-active and intelligent indicator films using eco-friendly CD@PANI composite.

Cellulose nanofibers (TEMPO-CNF; length: 5–50 μm; width: 10–20 nm) and sodium alginate (SA) were purchased from Moorim P&P Co., Ltd. (Ulsan, Korea) and Kanto Chemical Co., Inc. (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. Aniline, hydrogen chloride (HCl), and potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 13565), Listeria monocytogenes (ATCC 15313), Salmonella enterica (ATCC 14028), and Escherichia coli O157: H7 (ATCC 43895) were supplied by the Korean Collection for Type Culture (KCTC, Seoul, Korea). The cinnamon and fresh shrimp were purchased from a local supermarket in Seoul, Korea.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ preparation of polyaniline/CD composite (CD@PANI)
ce:section.ce:para: The polyaniline/CD composite (CD@PANI) was developed by a two-step process. In the first step, the cinnamon-based carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. For this purpose, cinnamon powder (2 g) was added to distilled water (50 mL), and then a 15 min sonication was performed to a homogeneous solution was obtained. The dispersion was transferred to a Teflon-lined reactor and treated at 190 °C for 9 h. The cooled CD solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. After that, the obtained solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm pore size membrane and the collected CD solution was stored at 4 °C before further experiments.

In the second step, the in situ preparation of polyaniline/CD composite (CD@PANI) was performed by polymerizing aniline into PANI in the presence of CD solution as a functionalizing agent. For this, 300 μL of concentrated HCl was added to 36 mL of CD solution (4.6 mg/mL) and stirred at room temperature for 10 min. Anilin (534 μL) was pipetted into the above solution and stirred under ice-bath conditions. Then, 30 mL of K2S2O8 solution (0.8 g in 30 mL distilled water) was added dropwise, and the above solution was stirred for 24 h to initiate the polymerization reaction. The resulting solution was then washed with ultrapure water several times by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min per cycle) until the supernatant reached neutral pH. Finally, the collected CD@PANI particles were dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for further use. For comparison, pure PANI particles were also prepared by using a similar approach without using CD solution.

Alginate/CNF (SA/CNF)-based composite films were prepared using the solution casting technique. Initially, 2 g of SA powder was dissolved in 100 mL distilled water (2 % w/v) under continuous magnetic stirring (2 h, 500rpm) at 90°C. Meanwhile, 2 g of CNF was added to 100 mL water and stirred at 40°C for 4 h. Then, the generated SA and CNF solutions were blended at 50°C for 90 min until a homogenous solution was obtained. Afterward, 1 wt% of each CD, pure PANI, and CD@PANI particles were separately added to the above SA/CNF mixture and stirred (500rpm) for 20 min at 50°C. The solutions were finally cast onto a rectangular Teflon-coated glass plate (30 cm × 24 cm) and dried at room temperature for 72 h. The prepared film samples were labeled based on the particle type as SA/CNF, SA/CNF/CD, SA/CNF/PANI, and SA/CNF/CD@PANI, respectively.
Detailed information about the characterization techniques used for the fabricated CD@PANI particles and SA/CNF-based films is presented in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity to acid and ammonia vapors
ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia and acetic acid served as model compounds to evaluate the response of SA/CNF-based films to biogenic amines [20]. Briefly, the film samples (3 cm × 3 cm) were attached above the beaker containing 30 mL of 99 % acetic acid or 0.8 M ammonia. The color parameters of films were recorded using a chromameter (Konica-Minolta, CR-400, Tokyo, Japan) after 15 min exposure to the solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smart packaging application of composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Seafood deteriorates during storage due to microbial growth and decomposition of proteins and other constituents, forming volatile biogenic amines. Therefore, protein-rich shrimp was used as a food model to evaluate the quality spoilage monitoring ability and maintainability of the prepared SA/CNF/CD@PANI composite films.

A square tag (3 cm × 3 cm) from each film sample was assembled into the lid of a sterilized container. Fresh shrimp (60 g) were placed in the container, sealed, and then stored at 28 °C for 24 h to conduct accelerated storage testing. The quantitative changes in color values [L (lightness), a (red-green), b (yellow-blue), and total color difference (ΔE)] of the indicator films and pH of shrimp samples in each group were measured using a colorimeter (Konica Minolta CR-400 Chromameter, Tokyo, Japan).

Shrimp samples (30 g) were packaged with 10 cm × 10 cm sterilized testing films (plastic wrap, SA/CNF, SA/CNF/CD, SA/CNF/PANI, and SA/CNF/CD@PANI) and kept at 4 °C for 15 days. The pH, hardness, total viable bacteria count (TVC), and lipid oxidation degree of packaged shrimps were measured every 5 days to indicate the quality of shrimp samples according to a previous report [3].

Each analysis was conducted in triplicate, and the results are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was utilized to assess the data, and the significance of each mean attribute was determined (p < 0.05) using Duncan's multiple range test, and the analysis was performed using Statistical Analysis Software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

The morphology of the prepared PANI and CD@PANI complex was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The SEM image shows the agglomerated irregular orbicular-like shape and broad size distribution of the PANI particles (Fig. 1 a ), which is a typical morphology of PANI synthesized through conventional methods [21,22]. The morphological characteristics of the CDs were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images reveal that the CDs exhibit a nearly spherical shape with uniform size distribution of 3.0 ± 0.4 nm (Fig. 1 b). The SEM image of CD@PANI particles showed that the integration of CDs with the PANI polymer resulted in a distinct change in morphology. The CD@PANI complex showed a more uniform and tubular structure (Fig. 1 c). Moreover, the CD@PANI structure exhibited an increased surface roughness compared with pure PANI, which confirmed that the CD nanostructures were uniformly decorated on the PANI particles. The obtained even structural morphology is expected to contribute positively to the functional properties of CD@PANI, which can enhance its performance in various applications.

Fig. 1d shows the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the prepared particles to analyze the surface functional groups and chemical structure of synthesized nanocomposites. The FTIR spectrum of pure CDs shows a broad absorption band around 3384 cm−1, which represents the OH stretching vibration. In contrast, the FTIR spectrum of pure PANI lacks this broad OH stretching band, highlighting its hydrophobicity. However, the distinct peak at 3242 cm−1, attributed to the NH stretching vibration, confirms the presence of amino groups in the PANI backbone and the polymerization of aniline monomers into polyaniline [23]. Additionally, the bands observed between 2924 and 2857 cm−1 are associated with the CH stretching of sp3 or sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. A prominent peak also appears at 1590 cm−1, which is attributed to the CC stretching vibration in the quinone ring of PANI structure or sp2-hybridized carbons. The absorption peaks centered at 1236, 1227, and 1149cm−1 represent the CN vibration of the quinonoid ring, the vibrations CN group of the phenyl ring, and the QC6H4 and NQN stretching vibrations of polymer structure, respectively [24,25]. For the CD@PANI complex, the peaks in the range of 3384–3242 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds of CDs and PANI, respectively. The similarity of the FTIR spectra of CD, PANI, and CD@PANI complex suggests the presence of CD and PANI in the CD@PANI structure, thus suggesting the successful development of CD@PANI complex. However, compared with pure PANI, the shifts of the FTIR peak of CD@PANI around 1590 cm−1 confirmed the interactions between PANI and CDs at the molecular level to form the CD@PANI complex structure [23].

Food oxidation starts with unsaturated fatty acids in the presence of catalysts such as enzymes, heat, or light, which initiates a self-accelerating chain reaction. Free radicals with unpaired electrons attack the double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids, degrading nucleic acids and proteins. This leads to the formation of conjugated dienes, hydroperoxides, aldehydes, and ketones, which produce rancid taste and odor. The use of antioxidants in active packaging can maintain the quality parameters and extend the storage life of foods rich in unsaturated fatty acids by inhibiting lipid oxidation [26]. Herin, the antioxidant capacity of synthesized CDs, PANI, and CD@PANI particles was measured using ABTS .+ and DPPH
scavenging assays (Fig. 2a).

The synthesized CDs exhibited a significant ability to neutralize ABTS and DPPH radicals with a concentration-dependent trend. At lower concentrations (12.5 μg/mL), CDs displayed radical scavenging percentages of approximately 58.3 % and 53.1 % for ABTS and DPPH assays, respectively. As the applied amount increased to 75 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL, the radical scavenging activity significantly (p < 0.05) improved and reached up to 100 % for ABTS and DPPH. This strong radical scavenging of CDs may be due to their high surface area and abundant functional groups available to interact with free radicals. CDs can act as neutralizing agents by transferring hydrogen from functional moieties, donating and accepting electrons from molecules, and forming radical adducts at sp2 carbon sites [27].

PANI alone also showed promising antioxidant activity, exhibiting 57.0 % radical scavenging activity for ABTS and 52.5 % for DPPH assays at the concentration of 12.5 μg/mL. With increased concentrations (75 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL), the performance enhanced to about 100 % for ABTS and DPPH. The intrinsic antioxidant property of PANI is likely due to the presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups and the electron delocalization ability that helps to stabilize the radicals [28]. The ABTS scavenging effect of PANI is primarily attributed to the delocalized π-electrons within the polyaniline backbone, which facilitate the electron transfer processes essential for neutralizing the radical. However, the hydrogen-donating ability of PANI can scavenge DPPH free radicals. Therefore, ABTS scavenging involves electron transfer, while DPPH involves both electron and hydrogen atom transfer [29]. Consequently, PANI demonstrates significant scavenging activity towards both ABTS and DPPH radicals. According to another explanation, the DPPH radicals oxidized the reduced (benzenoid) units of polyaniline to quinoid, effectively neutralizing the DPPH radicals [30]. Therefore, these distinct radical scavenging behaviors are influenced by the hydrophobic characteristics of PANI and the differing neutralization mechanisms of ABTS and DPPH radicals.

The CD@PANI complex exhibited superior antioxidant performance compared with individual CDs and PANI. At 12.5 μg/mL, CD@PANI exhibited a radical scavenging activity of 58.5 % for ABTS and 55.1 % for DPPH. When the concentration was increased to 50 μg/mL, the antioxidant activity significantly (p < 0.05) increased and reached 100 % for both ABTS and DPPH. The synergistic effect between CDs and PANI could be the reason for the great antioxidant role of CD@PANI. CDs provide a large surface area and active sites for radical interactions, while PANI contributes to electron delocalization and additional active sites, resulting in an amplified radical scavenging effect.

Food spoilage is a direct result of microbial growth and proliferation inside the packaging, which can be controlled by using antibacterial packaging films. The well diffusion approach was used to assay the antimicrobial action of CD, PANI, and CD@PANI against food-contaminating indicator strains of Gram-positive (L. monocytogenes and S. aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli and S. enterica) bacteria (Fig. 2b). CD, PANI, and CD@PANI exhibited inhibition zones of 4.1, 1.2, and 3.5 mm for E.coli and 7.5, 1.6, and 4.5 mm for S.enterica, respectively. However, the antibacterial ability of CD, PANI, and CD@PANI proved more effective action against Gram-positive strains, with values of 4.3, 0.9, and 3.2 mm for L. monocytogenes and 7.0, 3.9, and 5.8 mm for S. aureus. The results confirm the improved antibacterial effect of CD@PANI complex compared with pure PANI polymer.

It is reported that PANI polymer can induce the creation of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which stimulates the creation of hydroxyl radicals, resulting in biomolecule damage and possibly cell death. This may lead to the negligible antibacterial effect of PANI polymer [31]. Due to their high surface area and abundant surface functional moieties, CDs can promote reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation. The produced ROS (1O2, O2 •−, OH•, and HOO•) are highly reactive and attack the cytoplasmic membrane, peptidoglycan layer, DNA, lipids, and proteins within the bacteria. The interaction between CDs and the cell membrane causes the death of bacteria [32]. The combination of CDs and PANI creates a synergistic effect, where CDs enhance the dispersion and stability of PANI, while PANI provides a favorable environment for electron transfer and ROS generation. This synergistic effect disrupts the redox balance within the bacterial cell, further promoting cell damage and bacterial death, thereby maximizing the antibacterial potential of the hybrid material [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric response testing of particles to pH
ce:section.ce:para: Microbial degradation of proteins and amino acids in meat products leads to the release of basic compounds like ammonia and amines. The accumulation of these basic compounds increases the pH of meat products, which is applied as a crucial sensor of spoilage. As a result, the pH-sensitive colorimetric particles can be utilized as intelligent packaging compounds to indicate spoilage of protein-rich foods [34]. The color alteration function of CDs, PANI, and CD@PANI in buffer solutions in the pH range of 2–12 is depicted in Fig. 2c. PANI particles exhibited a green color at pH 2–6, blue at pH 7–10, and dark blue or purple at pH > 11. These color changes are due to the different oxidation states of PANI in a specific pH range driven by protonation and deprotonation reactions. Under moderately acidic conditions (pH 2–4), PANI exits in the form of emeraldine salt, which is a half-oxidized state. In this protonated configuration, PANI exhibits a distinctive green color and is considered the most stable form of the polymer [7]. Meanwhile, the CD nanoparticles did not present significant color change in various pHs of buffer solutions. However, as the pH increased from 7 to 12, the yellow color of CDs intensified and changed to brown, which is attributed to the protonation and deprotonation processes of surface functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl [35]. Interestingly, CD@PANI particles showed a similar color change tendency as PANI particles, indicating that the hybridization of CDs and PANI structure did not change or mask the color change ability of PANI. The obtained results suggest the potential of CD@PANI designed as a freshness sensor for packaged foods with increasing pH levels over time during storage.

FTIR spectroscopy can analyze potential chemical interactions among film components and detect any alterations in the film's chemical structure. The distinctive spectral peaks reveal the physical bonding and chemical interactions between various compounds [36]. The FTIR spectrum of neat SA/CNF film depicted the broad absorption peak at 3380 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic O-H/N-H stretching vibration, and the peak at 2935 corresponds to CH bond stretching (Fig. 3a). The sharp peak at 1600 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1 denotes the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate (COO−) of sodium alginate [37]. The peaks at 1160 and 1027 cm−1 are ascribed to the CC and C-O-C/C-O pyranose stretching (cellulose), respectively [38]. The peaks at 1088 and 864 cm−1 are associated with the vibration of CO bonds. The integration of CDs in the SA/CNF/CD and SA/CNF/CD@PANI films caused a shift of the OH and C–O–C stretching absorption peaks of the composite films to relatively higher wavenumbers. The minimal change observed in the IR spectra after CD incorporation is due to the overlapping of the stretching vibrations of CH, OH, CC, and CO within the almost same IR region. However, the additional characteristic peaks appearing at around 1300 and 1132 cm−1 in the SA/CNF/PANI and SA/CNF/CD@PANI films are attributed to the stretching vibration of CN (aromatic region CN and -N = quinoid = N bonds, respectively), which confirms the incorporation of PANI particles into the film samples. Therefore, the FTIR spectra of the composite films exhibited similar band patterns to the pure SA/CNF film, with only slight changes in peak positions and intensities due to the incorporation of CD, PANI, or CD@PANI fillers. These changes indicate the intermolecular bonding between the OH, NH, and -COOH groups of the SA/CNF polymer and the CD@PANI particles, confirming the successful incorporation of these particles into the polymer matrix [39].

XPS measurements were further conducted to analyze the chemical structure and elemental composition of the engineered SA/CNF-based films. The XPS survey spectra showed that the neat SA/CNF film was mainly composed of C (1 s, 62.61 %) and oxygen (O 1 s, 37.39 %) elements (Fig. 3b). However, excessive sulfur (S 2p, 0.4 %) and nitrogen (N 1 s, 1.88 %) peaks were detected in the SA/CNF/CD@PANI sample, indicating the presence of active functional groups of PANI and CDs within the polymer matrix. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of pure SA/CNF film was decomposed into four peaks at 283.5, 284.8, 285.4 and 287.3 eV, which correspond to C=C/C-C, CN, CO, and N-C=O bands, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3c [40]. The O 1 s spectra of both neat SA/CNF film and SA/CNF/CD@PANI film were deconvoluted into three distinct peaks at 529.8, 531.1, and 532.2 eV, which are assigned to binding energies of O=C-O, C-O-C/C-O-H, and O=C-O-C bonds (Fig. 3d). The N 1 s spectrum of the SA/CNF/CD@PANI film was resolved into three binding energy peaks at 398.3, 399.1, and 400.1 eV, which are assigned to CN, CN, and NH bonds, respectively [39,41] (Fig. 3e). The deconvoluted form of S 2p depicted the occurrence of –OSO3 2− (S 2P3/2), and –OSO3 2− (S 2P1/2) groups at binding energies of 166.1 and 167.4 eV, respectively [42] (Fig. 3f). These results demonstrated that the numerous surface functional groups of CDs and PANI helped in the fabrication of functional composite films through the cross-linking effect and various stable intermolecular interactions with the polymer matrix.

The microstructures of SA/CNF, SA/CNF/CD, SA/CNF/PANI, and SA/CNF/CD@PANI films were analyzed by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM). A smooth, uniform, crack-free, and wrinkle-free surface was observed for the neat SA/CNF film, confirming the good miscibility and compatibility between the two polymers. However, the presence of fine lines in the surface image is due to the fibrous nature of CNF in the polymer blend. The uniform distribution of CD nanoparticles resulted in the formation of dense and uniform microstructure, as shown in Fig. 4a. The SEM image of the SA/CNF/PANI film exhibited some white spots due to the agglomeration of the PANI particles induced by their hydrophobic nature. However, the CD@PANI loaded film (SA/CNF/CD@PANI) formed smooth, cohesive, and dense structures. The polymer chain of PANI consists of a conjugated network with an abundance of π electrons from the aromatic ring and a lone pair of electrons from the nitrogen structure, whereas the surface of CDs and SA/CNF comprise -OH, -C=O, and -COOH as major functional groups. Therefore, the abundance of reactive moieties enhances the compatibility of CD@PANI in the SA/CNF matrix owing to the strong hydrogen bonding interaction between the added functional fillers and binary polymer mixture. Meanwhile, due to the hydrogen bonding interaction, the uniform distribution of CD@PANI in the SA/CNF blend reduces the free volume and fluidity of polymer chains and enhances the network structure. The cross-sectional view of the internal structure of films confirmed that the morphology profile of SA/CNF was improved by the addition of CD@PANI filler (Fig. 4b). Since the uniform matrix facilitates a better allocation of stress [43], the observed morphology of SA/CNF/CD@PANI can contribute to stronger mechanical characteristics.

Transparency and the visible color of packaging materials enhance the overall appeal and attractiveness of a product by enabling consumers to evaluate the freshness and safety of the food [44]. The appearance and color parameters of the SA/CNF-based films are presented in Fig. 4c and Table 1 . The binary SA/CNF polymer film appeared colorless and transparent with color values of L = 91.0 ± 0.3, a = 4.2 ± 0.1, and b = 1.5 ± 0.1. The SA/CNF film incorporated with 1 wt% CD appeared pale yellow and was visually transparent with an L-value >80 and an enhanced b-value of 4.4 ± 0.1. However, when PANI particles were added, the film color changed to dark green because of the green color of PANI, which is correlated with the diminution of lightness and enhancement of the greenish parameter of the film (L = 46.9 ± 0.1, a = −3.3 ± 0.1, b = 1.1 ± 0.1a). The combination yellow color of the CDs and the green color of PANI resulted in a composite structure with a light green shade, inducing the increased b and L-values in the SA/CNF/CD@PANI compared to the SA/CNF/PANI film. However, despite the significant (p < 0.05) change in the total color difference (ΔE) of the SA/CNF/CD@PANI film (ΔE = 43.5 ± 0.3), the visually uniform morphology of the film allowed the monitoring of the food in the package.

Ultraviolet (UV) and visible light can induce photochemical reactions in foods, resulting in nutrient degradation, off-flavors, discoloration, and loss of sensory qualities. These detrimental effects necessitate the development of packaging materials that can effectively protect products from harmful light exposure. The transmittance of the prepared SA/CNF-based films in the UV–Vis spectral range (200–800 nm) is presented in Fig. 4d. The pure SA/CNF film showed high transmittance at the wavelengths of 200–800 nm, but the incorporation of fillers (CD, PANI, and CD@PANI) decreased the light transmittance of the film depending on the filler type. Moreover, the intensity reduction of the sample with PANI was much greater than that of the SA/CNF/CD film. The neat SA/CNF film showed 81.1 % transparency (T660) and 48.6 % UV-blocking capability (T280), indicating that the pure film is transparent and had minimal UV-blocking ability (Fig. 4d, Table 1). However, the visible light transmittance of the SA/CNF/CDs film at T660 remained unchanged, and its UV transmission at T280 significantly (p < 0.05) decreased from 48.6 % for the SA/CNF film to 13.6 %, indicating the substantial transparency and UV-blocking characteristics of the fabricated composite film. When PANI particles are integrated, the transmittance decreases significantly (p < 0.05) in both visible and UV regions, indicating their UV absorption while compromising transparency. Interestingly, the combination of CDs and PANI in SA/CNF/CD@PANI film showed a superb UV-blocking capability along with improved transparency.

Natural sunlight, particularly in the UV-A wavelength range of 320–400 nm, has significant penetration power, which can expedite lipid oxidation in food, leading to the development of undesirable flavors [45]. The designed SA/CNF/CD@PANI film presented an effective blocking efficiency of 88 % and strongly blocked 91.5 % of UV-B radiation (280–320 nm) (Fig. 4e). The imparted UV-blocking ability of CD@PANI is due to the fact that the added CDs and PANI fillers either reflect or absorb UV radiation, thereby hindering the passage of UV rays. Polyaniline is known to absorb UV light due to its π-conjugated backbone, which allows it to interact with photons in the UV region. The UV absorption of PANI is primarily attributed to the electronic transition between π and π* states within the polymer chain. However, the strong UV-blocking potential of CDs produced from biomass is attributed to their electronic structure and the comprised aromatic polyphenol component. Therefore, the SA/CNF/CD@PANI film with UV-screening capacity can be properly applied as UV-blocking food packaging to inhibit photocatalytic reactions in the packaged foods.

The mechanical characteristics of the films are influenced by the interaction between additives and plasticizers with the polymer matrix, demonstrating the films' durability, which is essential for preserving food quality during transportation and storage [46]. Therefore, the effects of CDs, PANI, and CD@PANI on the mechanical properties of the SA/CNF-based composite films were evaluated by measuring the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) of the films, as detailed in Table 2 and Fig. S1. The TS and EB of pure SA/CNF film were 73.0 ± 2.4 MPa and 6.3 ± 1.0 %, respectively. TS and EB of the films increased to 75.6 ± 3.0 MPa and 8.0 ± 1.2 % by the addition of CD particles. The high surface area and abundant functional groups on the CD surface enable efficient cross-linking and effective interfacial interactions with the polymer matrix. This facilitates the stress transfer from the polymer matrix to the fillers, thus improving the mechanical properties of the film [47]. However, the addition of PANI particles decreased the TS of the film to 65.7 ± 1.9 MPa and increased the EB to 8.7 ± 1.4 %. The decrease in the strength of the SA/CNF/PANI film can be attributed to the agglomerated PANI particles (Fig. 4a), which reduced the interaction sites with the polymer matrix and weakened the hydrogen bonding. The SA/CNF/CD@PANI film showed enhanced strength (TS = 82.5 ± 3.3 MPa) and reduced flexibility (EB = 8.1 ± 1.5 %) compared to the SA/CNF/PANI film. These changes may be related to the effective compatibility of CD@PANI with polymer background, as described in section 3.2.2. The compatibility and uniform dispersion of CD@PANI particles increase the interfacial interaction and improve the load distribution, making a strong film. Meanwhile, the uniform distribution of CD@PANI in the polymer blend reduces the free volume and fluidity of polymer chains, which decreases the flexibility of the films [48]. These results suggest that the integration of the CD@PANI complex promotes the intrinsic mechanical characteristic of SA/CNF film.

WVP is an important parameter for evaluating water vapor barrier properties of packaging film because moisture loss from fresh produce and moisture absorption from dry foods can accelerate spoilage [49]. Moreover, when the film is adsorbed and swollen by water vapor, the gas permeability increases, which can affect the reaction of the film with external acidic and alkaline gases and the gas-sensing ability of smart films [50]. Water vapor permeation into the film is affected by the hydrophilic and hydrophobic balance of the matrix and the microstructure of the film [51]. As presented in Table 2, the WVP of the neat SA/CNF film was 8.2 ± 0.4 × 10−10 g.m/m2.Pa.s, which slightly decreased to 7.9 ± 0.1 × 10−10 g.m/m2.Pa.s with the addition of 1 wt% CDs due to the combined effect of the hydrophilic structure of CDs and the intermolecular interactions of the film matrix. The film was further compressed due to the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions between CDs and the film matrix, which prevented water vapor from passing through the film [52]. However, the free hydroxyl groups of hydrophilic CDs assisted the diffusion of water vapor through the film [53]. Thus, the WVP decreased slightly due to the combination of the above reasons. The WVP of SA/CNF/PANI film showed no significant (p < 0.05) change compared to the pristine SA/CNF film, which may be due to the disturbed hydrophobicity effect of PANI particles by their inappropriate distribution in the matrix. However, the WVP of SA/CNF/CD@PANI film showed a more substantial decrease, reaching 7.3 ± 0.3 × 10−10 g.m/m2.Pa.s. The reduced WVP can be related to the distorted path of water molecules in the film, which was formed through the dense network structure of the film with the help of the uniform distribution of CD@PANI filler [54].

The water contact angle (WCA), which reflects the adhesion and cohesion between the film surface and water, is a crucial characteristic for assessing the surface wettability and hydrophobicity of packaging materials [55]. The neat SA/CNF film exhibited a low WCA of 42.05 ± 1.7°, indicating a hydrophilic surface (Table 2 and Fig. 4f). The WCA of the film containing 1 wt% CD increased slightly to 44.7 ± 1.5°. The inherent hydrophilicity of CDs suggests that the film will be more hydrophilic, but the wettability of SA/CNF/CD is slightly reduced due to the limited availability of hydrophilic groups in the films caused by the -OH interactions of CDs and polymer. At 1 wt% of PANI particles, the maximum WCA of the composite film reached 58.3 ± 1.8°, which is due to the hydrophobicity of PANI filler. The WCA of the SA/CNF/CD@PANI film was lower than that of the CNF/PANI film but significantly higher than that of the neat SA/CNF film with a value of 50.1 ± 1.4°. The substantial increase in WCA can be ascribed to the intermolecular interactions between CD@PANI and the film matrix, which promote molecular reorientation and increase the cohesion of the composite film. As a result, the availability of hydrophilic groups, such as free hydroxyl groups, in the SA/CNF/CD@PANI film decreased, thereby limiting their interactions with water molecules [40].

Fig. 5a-c shows the differences in antibacterial performance of SA/CNF-based composite films against Gram-positive and Gram-negative food-borne microorganisms using the colony count method. The agar medium of SA/CNF film was covered with bacteria for all tested bacteria, indicating that the pure SA/CNF film didn't show any antimicrobial activity compared with the blank control (without any treatment) sample. The bacterial survival rate (bacteria colonies in the plate) decreased substantially with the incorporation of CD particles into the films, with inhibitory effects of 62.3, 94.6, and 87.1, 100 % for E. coli, S. enterica, L. monocytogenes, and S. aureus, respectively. The SA/CNF/PANI film demonstrated some antibacterial effects, with antibacterial rates of 10.5, 17.4, 33.6, and 13.8 % against E. coli, S. enterica, L. monocytogenes, and S. aureus, respectively. The SA/CNF/CD@PANI film presented the strongest antibacterial ability, exhibiting an inhibitory effect of 33.6, 49.6, 92.2, and 60.6 % against E. coli, S. enterica, L. monocytogenes, and S. aureus, respectively. The mechanism of cell death may be related to the release of ROS and the role of the surface charge of CD@PANI complex. ROS generation by PANI and CD@PANI has an important role in the eradication of bacteria. The unpaired or free electrons of CD@PANI can react with the dissolved oxygen in the solution to produce additional ROS. In addition, the synthesized CD@PANI has a negative surface charge due to the presence of various functional groups [56]. This indicates that interactions such as van der Waals and London dispersion forces have a major role in the eradication of the tested bacteria.

ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging techniques were employed to measure the antioxidant ability of the formulated films (Fig. 5d). The radical scavenging effect of the pure SA/CNF film was the lowest among all the film samples, i.e., 6.7 % for ABTS and 4.6 % for DPPH, which could be attributed to the aromatic groups in the polymer matrix, which can trap free radicals [24]. For the films integrated with active particles, the ABTS.+ and DPPH scavenging ability was remarkably improved, i.r., 100 % and 64.0 % for CDs and 42.1 % and 31.6 % for PANI, respectively. The observed antioxidant activity trend may be due to the combined effect of the different antioxidant efficiencies and release rates of CD and PANI particles in aqueous solution and less polar solvent methanol. However, the radical scavenging rate of the SA/CNF/CD@PANI film reached the maximum values of 100 % for ABTS and 69.2 % for DPPH, respectively, due to the electron-donating and electron-accepting abilities of CDs and PANI structures. The synergistic interaction between CDs and PANI enhances the radical scavenging ability of the film. CDs are known to rapidly release and quench free radicals with high efficiency in aqueous solution, whereas PANI particles contribute to sustained antioxidant activity, particularly in less polar solvents such as methanol. This complementary behavior results in more effective and sustained antioxidant activity. Saikia et al. (2018) prepared a polyaniline nanofiber/carbon dots nanohybrid as an efficient fluorimetric sensor for As (III) [33]. The developed nanohybrid exhibited remarkable antioxidant activity due to the combined effect of PANI and CDs. The proposed mechanism suggests that the nitrogen atoms of PANI have a lone pair of electrons, which can be easily delocalized in the quinoid-benzoid conjugated structure. The oxygen atoms of CDs also contain a lone pair of electrons. The graphitic structure of CDs can stabilize these electrons through a similar delocalization process. Therefore, the fabricated CD@PANI complex can be applied as an effective antioxidant filler in biopolymer packaging films to prevent the loss of essential nutrients, inhibit enzymatic browning, and reduce the formation of off-flavor compounds caused by food oxidation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH response and sensitivity to ammonia and acetic acid vapors
ce:section.ce:para:
The pH responsiveness is an important ability of smart films that reflects the applicability of the developed films as freshness monitoring labels. Fig. 6
a shows the color response function of the SA/CNF-based labels in various pH solutions (pH 2–12). As expected, the neat SA/CNF film was transparent and colorless when exposed to different pH buffers. However, the color of the SA/CNF/CD film slightly turned from light yellow to dark yellow when the pH was raised to 12, which correlated to the colorimetric response of the CD solution. The color of the SA/CNF/PANI film was green at pH 2–6. When pH was altered from 7 to 12, the color of PANI-based films faded little by little and turned into a blue hue. The SA/CNF/PANI film showed a purple tone color at pH 12. The findings suggested PANI incorporation can significantly impart pH sensitivity to SA/CNF-based films, which is attributed to the structural transformation of PANI particles when the solution was changed from acidic to alkaline. The comparison of the SA/CNF/CD@PANI film with the SA/CNF/PANI film demonstrated that incorporating CDs did not interfere with the films' color-shifting properties and even marginally improved the colorimetric response of the SA/CNF/PANI film.

Moreover, the color parameters (L, a, and b) and total color difference (ΔE) of the labels immersed in the buffer solutions are depicted in Fig. 6
b,c. The a-values of SA/CNF/PANI and SA/CNF/CD@PANI film gradually increased with increasing pH values, and in other words, the greenness of the films weakened. Concurrently, the b-values decreased in the alkaline pH region, indicating a shift towards a blue shade of the PANI-incorporated films. The ΔE value of the SA/CNF/CD@PANI label immersed in pH buffer was greater than 6.5, indicating that the color change is easily perceivable by the human eye and is in good agreement with the Japan Industrial Standard (JIS) color tolerance grade C [57]. These results suggest that the developed smart films can potentially be applied as pH sensors.

The spoilage process of seafood and meat generally generates volatile ammonia and amines because of the degradation of proteins and fats by enzymes [58]. Therefore, the ammonia-sensitive smart films can be applied as a freshness sensor of seafood. The sensitivity of the designed films to ammonia and acid vapors was evaluated to simulate the substances released during seafood spoilage ( Fig. 6
d). The color of SA/CNF/PANI and SA/CNF/CD@PANI labels clearly changed from green to blue after exposing to ammonia vapors for 10 min. The absorbed ammonia on the surface of films bonded with H2O to form ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) and was subsequently hydrolyzed to OH− and NH4 + [58]. This produced an alkaline environment, which induced the transformation from PANI-emeraldine salt to PANI-emeraldine base, and the color changed from green to blue. However, no noticeable color change occurred for the SA/CNF/PANI and SA/CNF/CD@PANI film exposed to acetic acid (CH3COOH), suggesting that the oxidation states of PANI did not change under acidic conditions.

Moreover, the a-values and the b-values of SA/CNF/PANI and SA/CNF/CD@PANI films significantly (p < 0.05) increased, which means the color of films turned from green to blue after exposure to NH3 gas ( Fig. 6
e,f). Furthermore, the color transition of the labels was visually distinguishable as the ΔE value criterion exceeded 6.5. The significant color change of the SA/CNF/CD@PANI label detected by the naked eye suggests the potential use of the film as a quality biomarker of seafood.

Shrimp spoilage is a major problem for fishermen and processors. Preservation of shrimp quality is crucial to maintain market value and control costs. Shrimp is prone to spoilage due to its high moisture content, free amino acids, and non‑nitrogen compounds, which are easily used as nutrients. Shrimp spoilage is associated with microbial degradation, auto-oxidation of unsaturated fatty acids, and endogenous enzyme activity. The neutral pH and high water activity of shrimp further increase their susceptibility to spoilage, producing volatile compounds such as ammonia, dimethylamine, methylamine, and trimethylamine, which are referred to as total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N). Consumption of spoiled shrimp can cause health issues such as gastroenteritis and septicemia. Moreover, spoilage microbes produce biogenic amines (e.g., histamine, tyramine, cadaverine, putrescine) through decarboxylation of free amino acids, which pose additional health risks, including allergenic and carcinogenic effects. These volatile compounds are signs of shrimp spoilage, which gradually accumulate at the top of the package and can be detected using ammonia-sensitive films. The potential use of SA/CNF-based films as smart sensors was explored through the monitoring of shrimp freshness (Fig. 7
). The transparent SA/CNF film did not show any color change as expected during the deterioration of the shrimp samples. The SA/CNF/CD film gradually changed color from light to dark yellow as the samples began to deteriorate during storage. Further, the spoilage of shrimp was determined by monitoring the TVB-N values for the shrimp samples at 0 h and 24 h. It was found that shrimp tend to possess high TVB-N values under unfavorable packaged conditions. Fig. S2 (supporting information) displays the TVB-N (mg/l00 g) values of fresh shrimp at 0 h fall in the range of 9.8 ± 0.76 mg/l00 g. However, the sharp increase in TVB-N value to 28.9 ± 1.21 mg/l00 g after 24 h of storage at 28 °C verified the deterioration of the packed shrimps (Fig. S2). According to published reports, once the TVB-N value reaches 25 mg/100 g or more, the organic amines start releasing duo to break down of dietary proteins [59]. As a result, a food is deemed spoiled and should not be eaten if its TVB-N level is 25 mg/l00g or above [35].

Regarding the relationship between the film colors and shrimp freshness, the spoilage of the shrimp led to the accumulation of volatile amines during storage, which raised the pH inside the packaging, and the green color of SA/CNF/CD@PANI film with a pH-responsive nature changed to blue/purple, which can be clearly visualized with the unaided eyes. This visual color change of the SA/CNF/CD@PANI film from green to purple confirms the spoilage of the packaged shrimp and declares it unfit for consumption.

In addition, the color change of indicator films was quantitatively confirmed by the significant increase in the color parameters, especially increasing the b-value of the film (Fig. 7b, c). After 24 h of storage, the films containing PANI particles (SA/CNF/PANI and SA/CNF/CD@PANI) exhibited a color shift from green to blue, correlating to the color change of films exposed to ammonia. This color transition was quantitatively validated by an enhanced a-value and diminished b-value, which indicated a shift from the initial greenish to blue. Correspondingly, the color change of the shrimp from gray to red indicates that the shrimp decomposed and produced volatile nitrogenous compounds. The observed color change of the designed smart label is visually distinguishable, confirming the potential of the film to detect accumulated volatile alkaline gases.

The pH and TBARS contents of shrimp samples were further determined to verify the accuracy of the SA/CNF-based labels. The TBARS value was evaluated by quantifying the content of malondialdehyde (MDA), a byproduct of lipid oxidation. MDA is generated when oxygen radicals target unsaturated fatty acids in the lipids of living organisms [60]. An increase in the TBARS value was observed for all the samples as the storage time progressed (Fig. 8b). This occurs because primary oxidation products gradually convert into more stable secondary oxidation compounds. The TBARS values increased significantly (p < 0.05) and reached 1.3 and 1.21 mM MDA/Kg for PE and SA/CNF packs, respectively. However, the TBARS values of shrimp packaged with SA/CNF/CD film and SA/CNF/PANI film were 0.64 and 0.79 mM MDA/Kg after 15 days, which is due to reduced oxidation of shrimps through the antioxidant action of CD and PANI particles. The best efficacy of the developed films in reducing shrimp oxidation was associated with a much lower TBARS value (0.5 mM MDA/Kg throughout 15 days storage) obtained for the SA/CNF/CD@PANI film treatment.

The spoilage of shrimp is marked by a significant increase in pH due to bacterial growth and loss of texture as muscle proteins and connective tissues decompose. The initial pH value of shrimp was 6.7. The pH values of the PE, SA/CNF, and SA/CNF/PANI groups increased rapidly throughout the storage period and were significantly (p < 0.05) higher than those of the other groups (Fig. 8c). The shrimp in the SA/CNF/CD@PANI group maintained the lowest pH value (pH = 7.15) and growth rate. The increase in pH is strongly correlated with the bacterial growth in the shrimp. As the bacterial population grows, the production of alkaline byproducts accelerates, which leads to a greater increase in pH.

The licensed total colony count of meat products is 7 log CFU/g, according to the International Commission on Microbiological Specifications for Food (ICMSF) [61]. A TVC value of 2.5 was observed for fresh shrimp samples. The TVC of shrimp samples presented an elevated trend with increasing storage time (Fig. 8d). However, the PE, SA/CNF, and SA/CNF/PANI groups exceeded the limit on day 10, and the TVC of the SA/CNF/CD group exceeded the limit on day 15. These findings propose that the SA/CNF/CD@PANI active film can efficiently slow down the growth of bacteria in packaged shrimp.

In addition, high pH levels cause the breakdown of muscle proteins and connective tissues, resulting in loss of texture. The shrimp become soft, mushy, and unappetizing, making them unsuitable for consumption. The firmness of fresh shrimp was 26.5 N, and it decreased rapidly within 10 days for the PE, SA/CNF, and SA/CNF/PANI groups (Fig. 8e). The decrease in firmness of shrimps is attributed to the protease activity released from the microorganisms that cause muscle degradation [62]. On the other hand, SA/CNF/CD and SA/CNF/CD@PANI films delayed the texture loss and effectively maintained the firmness values during storage up to 15 days.

These data showed that the quality parameters of shrimp samples were in good agreementwith the color changes observed on the sensor labels, validating the accuracy of the films in dynamic maintenance and monitoring of shrimp freshness.

The use of a CA@PANI-incorporated SA/CNF-based smart food packaging system can provide promising benefits by delaying microbial contamination, which will reduce the problem of food waste. In addition to greatly reducing foodborne microbes and increasing shelf life, the SA/CNF/CD@PANI-based packaging system can also fulfill consumer prospects about packaged food's high quality, convenience, freshness, safety, flavor, aroma, texture and color. The use of biomass-derived CD and PANI-based functional filler incorporation in the biopolymer matrix possesses favorable biodegradable characteristics that will not harm the environment. Consequently, it has the potential to be employed in intelligent food packaging for the safe transport of fresh packaging goods from production units to supermarkets.

In this study, a novel SA/CNF-based smart film was designed by incorporating CD@PANI complex into SA/CNF blend polymer for food preservation and visual freshness monitoring. The CD@PANI particles were fabricated by in situ polymerization of aniline in the presence of CDs. The CD@PANI exhibited good compatibility with the SA/CNF matrix, as verified by XPS, FTIR, and FE-SEM investigations, and significantly promoted the mechanical properties, hydrophobicity, and light barrier of the film. Moreover, the SA/CNF/CD@PANI film demonstrated significant pH sensitivity and distinct color changes under different pHs, which were visible to the naked eye. Volatile amines accumulate during the spoilage of protein-rich foods, so the remarkable color change from green to blue in SA/CNF/CD@PANI film when exposed to ammonia vapors can be used as a quality tracking marker for packaged shrimp products. In addition, the shrimp packaging test performed at 4 °C for 15 days showed the SA/CNF/CD@PANI film could delay shrimp oxidation and the growth of foodborne bacteria, thereby maintaining the texture and quality of packaged shrimp and extending the shelf life of shrimp. These findings highlight the potential of CD@PANI as an intelligent and active filler for developing high-demand smart packaging films to track the freshness of animal-based protein foods and retard the spoilage of the food process. However, future development of CD@PANI-incorporated biopolymer-based nanocomposite packaging can regulate and monitor the internal environment of food products by detecting volatile chemicals and microbes within the packaging systems that can help maintain optimal external conditions.

Zohreh Riahi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ajahar Khan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing. Gye Hwa Shin: Writing – review & editing. Jun Tae Kim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
